parts.instructions: For each question, choose the correct answer.

questions.text:
Harry,
We're out of milk. We'll need some tonight, so can you stop somewhere on your way home from college? Dinner is at 6.
Mum
options: Harry's mother is finding out what Harry wants for dinner.
Harry's mother is telling Harry not to be late home tonight.
Harry's mother is asking Harry to buy something for her.

questions.text:
Sammy,
The movie at the film club tonight is very popular. So shall I see you at our usual meeting place, but a bit earlier?
Tom
questions.question: What does Tom want to change?
options: the place where he'll meet Sammy
the movie he and Sammy will see

questions.text:
NOTICES
Harwood College Picnic
Saturday 1 p.m.
Sports field (Gym if raining)
Harwood students – free
Guests – £5
Fantastic band!
Food included
options: Everyone who comes to the picnic should bring some food.
The picnic won't happen if the weather is bad.
Some people who come to the picnic must pay.

questions.text:
To: Emily
From: Carol
I'm driving to college every day this term. If you can pay a bit for petrol, I'll take you too. Parking's free.
questions.question: Carol wants Emily
options: to share the cost of driving to college.
to give her a lift to college some days.
to help pay for parking at college.

questions.text:
City Station
Bad weather!
Trains working as usual
Be careful of ice on platforms
questions.question: What is the problem today?
options: The station might need to close.

questions.text:
Emma,
Any ideas for Frank's birthday present? I can get something at the shopping centre on my way to his party on Saturday.
Liz
questions.question: What is Liz doing in this text message?

parts.instructions: For each question, choose the correct answer.

people.name: Eugenia
people.description: My mother and father both play the violin in an orchestra, so that's what they began teaching me to play, even before I went to school. Now I've finished university and I'm a member of the same orchestra. I believe it's important for everyone to go to see music being played. Tickets are expensive, but it's a much better experience than just listening to recorded music. Over the years, I've won a few violin competitions and I love making music with my friends.

people.name: Laura
people.description: When I was a child, my father had a café and the only music there, and in our house, was jazz on the radio. But when I was 12, I went on a school trip and heard classical guitar for the first time. I loved it, and my mother bought me a guitar and paid for lessons. My parents couldn't believe it when I came first in a national guitar competition. I've left university now, but I'm still learning new things every day. There's so much information online.

people.name: Ellie
people.description: I'm now playing in my university orchestra and taking classes there in writing music, which is what I want to do as a career. I love using the internet to discuss things I'm working on with other musicians. I was lucky to be able to have classical violin lessons at school and, by the time I was eight, I was in the children's orchestra and playing in local competitions. In my opinion, more schools should offer music lessons. Music is so important.

questions.text: Who thinks not enough music is taught in schools?
Who grew up in a home without much classical music?
Who likes to share ideas about music with people online?
Who says she thinks people should go to concerts?
Who started learning to play an instrument after she was given one?
Who is now studying music at university?
Who says her parents were surprised when she won a competition?

parts.instructions: For each question, choose the correct answer.
parts.passage:
Fifteen-year-old British student Jonathan Davis has always loved learning about the past. So, when he saw a newspaper article about people who enjoy looking in the countryside for very old things made of metal, such as coins, he wanted to try the hobby himself.

To get started, he had to have something called a detector. You hold this machine over the ground, and it makes a loud noise if there is metal there. He bought a small one and began using it near the village where he lives.

He didn't find much for the first few months. Then, one day, he and a friend were looking in a field. 'We were a bit bored,' he says. 'But suddenly, I found a few bits of metal lying in the grass. As soon as I saw them, I had a feeling they might be really old, so I showed them to my friend. We kept looking, and together we found 200 more pieces that day!'

Jonathan took the pieces to a museum. The staff there told him they were almost two thousand years old. They explained that at that time people sometimes cut silver bowls and cups into small pieces, and then used the pieces as money.

The museum later had a special exhibition of the silver pieces, which Jonathan went to. He said, 'It was really exciting to see what the people at the museum were able to do to clean the pieces of silver and find out exactly what they are. They look really different now. I'm so pleased I found them and that now other people can see them too.'

questions.text: What do we learn about Jonathan from the first paragraph?
options: He has lots of unusual hobbies.

questions.text: What is the writer doing in the second paragraph?
What does Jonathan say in the third paragraph about the metal pieces?
options: It took several days to find all of them.
He was pleased when his friend found them.

questions.text: What did the staff at the museum tell Jonathan about the metal pieces?
options: They were not as old as Jonathan thought.
It's a shame they were so small.
In the past, people used them to buy and sell things.

questions.text: How did Jonathan feel when he visited the exhibition?
options: surprised so many people came to see the silver
happy he could share the silver with others
sad that the silver looked so different

parts.instructions: For each question, choose the correct answer.
parts.passage:
The African-American writer Toni Morrison was born in 1931. When she was growing up, her parents (19) ______ her about African-American history and music. She often said that (20) ______ stories was an important (21) ______ of her family's daily life. She also loved books and spent a lot of her free time reading.

In 1949, Toni Morrison went to Howard University in Washington, D.C. to study English. After (22) ______ her course, her first job was teaching. She wrote her first book, The Bluest Eye, in 1970. Her later books were very popular, (23) ______ Beloved, which is now a well-known film. Toni Morrison (24) ______ many national and international prizes, including the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1993. She became the first black woman ever to receive this.
